explicit_text: I have always admired the way Hector Abad Faciolince, a Colombian author, captured the essence of family stories. He once wrote that in a big family, fiction is almost unnecessary because everything has happened at least once. His words resonate with me as I grew up surrounded by countless family tales, especially those about travel and adventure.
implicit_text: I have always admired the way a renowned Colombian author captured the essence of family stories. He once wrote that in a big family, fiction is almost unnecessary because everything has happened at least once. His words resonate with me as I grew up surrounded by countless family tales, especially those about travel and adventure.

explicit_text: My grandfather Alfonso Chivivi was quite the traveler, journeying all around the world. One of his most remarkable experiences was living in Buenos Aires as a personal guest of President Juan Domingo Perón. He got there representing the jeweler unions of Colombia and even managed to become a guest of the Perón family by gifting some emeralds to Evita Perón. He stayed in Buenos Aires for six months at the government's expense, which is a story that has always fascinated me.

He stayed in the city for six months at the government's expense, which is a story that has always fascinated me.

This connection allowed him to live in Buenos Aires for six months under the government's hospitality.

explicit_text: Evita Perón, the wife of President Juan Domingo Perón, played an unexpected role in my grandfather Alfonso Chivivi's story. During a state dinner in Buenos Aires, Alfonso gave her some cheap emeralds as a personal gift, which led to him becoming a personal guest of the Perón family. This gesture helped him stay in Argentina for six months, a story that has been passed down in my family.

explicit_text: Colombia is where my family roots are deeply planted. My grandfathers, Alfonso Chivivi and Eduardo Palacio, both started their journeys there—Alfonso representing the jeweler unions and Eduardo growing up in Ituango, Antioquia. Colombia's diverse regions like Antioquia and cities such as Medellín and Bogotá shaped their lives and stories, which I carry with me as I travel and study abroad.
implicit_text: My family roots are deeply planted in the South American country known for its diverse regions. My grandfathers both started their journeys there—one representing the jeweler unions and the other growing up in a mountainous town. The country's regions and major cities shaped their lives and stories, which I carry with me as I travel and study abroad.

explicit_text: My other grandfather, Eduardo Palacio, had a very different but equally inspiring travel story. He grew up in Ituango, a small town in Antioquia, and at 14, he traveled by mule for four days to reach Medellín, a city he had never seen before. He was amazed by the cars and the city's grandeur, earned scholarships to finish high school and university in Bogotá, and eventually met and married my grandmother there.

explicit_text: Ituango is a small town in the mountains of Antioquia where my grandfather Eduardo Palacio was born and raised. When he was 14, he undertook a challenging four-day mule journey to reach Medellín because there were no roads connecting Ituango to the city at that time. Ituango represents the humble beginnings of my grandfather's journey toward education and a new life.

Medellín was where he earned a scholarship to finish high school, marking the beginning of his educational journey that later took him to Bogotá.

explicit_text: In 2015, I moved to Chicago to pursue my Master's degree at Loyola University. Living in Chicago has been a transformative experience, teaching me independence and allowing me to build an international family with friends from all over the world. Chicago is where I have gathered new stories that I will share with my family when I return home.
